coredata.dc:title: Phytoconstituent-derived zingerone nanoparticles disrupt the cell adhesion mechanism and suppress cell motility in melanoma B16F10 cells

coredata.dc:description: Combining phytochemicals and nanotechnology to improve the unfavorable innate properties of phytochemicals and develop them into potent nanomedicines to enhance antitumor efficacy has become a novel strategy for cancer chemoprevention. Melanoma is the most aggressive, metastatic, and deadly disease of the primary cutaneous neoplasms. In this study, we fabricated phytoconstituent-derived zingerone nanoparticles (NPs) and validated their effects on cell adhesion and motility in melanoma B16F10 cells. Our data indicated that zingerone NPs significantly induced cytotoxicity and anti-colony formation and inhibited cell migration and invasion. Moreover, zingerone NPs dramatically interfered with the cytoskeletal reorganization and markedly delayed the period of cell adhesion. Our results also revealed that zingerone NPs-mediated downregulation of MMPs (matrix metalloproteinases) activity is associated with inhibiting cell adhesion and motility. We further evaluated the effects of zingerone NPs on Src/FAK /Paxillin signaling, our data showed that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited the protein activities of Src, FAK, and Paxillin, indicating that they play important roles in zingerone NP-mediated anti-motility and anti-invasion in melanoma cells. Accordingly, the phytoconstituent-zingerone NPs can strengthen the inhibition of tumor growth, invasion, and metastasis in malignant melanoma. Altogether, these multi-pharmacological benefits of zingerone NPs will effectively achieve the purpose of melanoma prevention and invasion inhibition.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Zingerone NP generation and cell culture
Effects of zingerone NPs on cell viability, proliferation, and the in vitro tumorigenicity of melanoma B16F10 cells
Zingerone NP-disturbed cell adhesion contributes to anti-invasiveness
Zingerone NP-mediated downregulation of Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling was involved in inhibited cell adhesion and cell motility and metastatic suppression

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Scheme of the as-fabricated Phytoconstituents-derived zingerone NPs. The nanotetramer architecture of zingerone NPs has resulted from a hemiketal reaction that condensed the alcohol group with the adjacent ketone group between two zingerone compounds.

The effects of nanosized-zingerone on cytotoxicity and cell proliferation in melanoma B16F10 cell line. B16F10 cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h. (A) Microscopy observation of cellular morphology changes and cell growth inhibition was monitored using an inverted microscope assay. Scale bar: 100 µm. (B) Next, an MTT assay was used to detect the effect of zingerone NPs on cytotoxicity. (C) The effect of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs on cell proliferation was monitored and detected using an image monitoring assay at different times- and dose courses. (D) Further, both zingerone and zingerone NPs mediated cell confluence changes were analyzed using GraphPad Prism software. Our results indicated that zingerone NPs exhibited markedly cytotoxicity and anti-cell proliferation in melanoma B16F10 cell line compared to zingerone treatment. Data were expressed as the mean ± SEM. * p<0.05, ** p<0.01.

Effect of zingerone NPs on in vitro colony formation of melanoma B16F10 cell line. (A) Cells (2000 cells/well) were incubated in various concentrations of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs (0, 10, 25, 50, 100, and 200 µM) and allowed to form colonies for 7–10 days and subjected to crystal violet staining assay. (B) The relative colony numbers were counted and recorded using prism statistical software. (C) Colony survival activity was further determined by measuring the dissolved crystal violet at an absorption wavelength of 595 nm using a microplate reader sassy. All data are expressed as the mean ± SEM of three experiments. * p<0.05, ** p<0.01.

Evaluated the effects of nanosized-zingerone on the cytoskeleton reorganization of melanoma B16F10 cells. (A) Cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs NPs for 24 h, and the cell morphology changes were observed and recorded through staining with F-actin phalloidin and recorded using confocal microscopy assay. Scale bar: 50 µm. (B) zingerone NPs-mediated elongation of the cell membrane was further measured and analyzed using a ZEN 3.1 image-processing software. Cell length was counted at least 30–50 cells from each group. **p< 0.01 compared with indicated groups.

Effects of nanosized-zingerone on cell adhesive ability toward extracellular matrix coating condition. Cells were suspended in a medium containing zingerone and/or zingerone NPs and seeded in a culture plate coated (A) without or (B) with gelatin for 60 min. Cells were then stained with F-actin phalloidin and then analyzed the adhesive ability using inverted fluorescent microscopy. On the other hand, Cells were fixed with 4 % PFA and stained with a crystal violet solution. The stained dye dissolved from cells cultured in a plate coated (C) without or (D) with gelatin was next subjected to detect the absorbance of OD 595 nm. *p< 0.05, and **p< 0.01 compared with indicated groups.

Characterized the effects of zingerone NPs on cell invasiveness and MMPs activity. (A) Representative pictures of invaded cells after receiving various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs administration for 48 h. Scale bar: 100 µm. (B) The number of invaded cells was calculated from three different fields for each experimental group. (C) The effect of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs on protein activities of MMP-9 and MMP-2 were analyzed using a Zymography assay. (D) The gelatin-digested intensity in both MMP-9 and MMP-2 was further quantification and analyzed using Image-Pro Plus software. All data were expressed as mean ± SEM. *p< 0.05, and **p< 0.01 compared with indicated groups.

Zingerone NPs disturbed cellular mechanical-associated proteins assembly in B16F10 cells. The effects of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs on the activity statuses of mechanical-associated proteins, such as (A) Src, (C) FAK, and (E) paxillin were achieved using western blotting assay. The protein activity of (B) Src, (D) FAK, and (F) paxillin were further quantitated and analyzed using GraphPad Prism 5.0 software. All data were expressed as mean ± SEM. **p< 0.01 compared with indicated groups.

The schematic presentation showed the effects of zingerone NPs on cell focal adhesion and cell motility in melanoma B16F10 cells. As shown in (panel I.), in adequate condition, cells were well adhered to or interacted with extracellular matrix components, and then upregulated the Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling cascades. In response to the survival signaling, cells subsequently carried out the processes of cell proliferation, migration, invasion, and tumorigenesis. In contrast, when cells were treated with zingerone NPs, zingerone NPs significantly elicited downregulation of the Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling and inhibition of FAK-mediated PI3K/Akt signaling. These outcomes subsequently may contribute to poor cell adhesion, dysregulation of cytoskeleton organization, failure of cell viability, and finally, superiorly suppressed cell viability, cell proliferation, cell invasion, and tumorigenesis (panel II.).

head.ce:title: Phytoconstituent-derived zingerone nanoparticles disrupt the cell adhesion mechanism and suppress cell motility in melanoma B16F10 cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Combining phytochemicals and nanotechnology to improve the unfavorable innate properties of phytochemicals and develop them into potent nanomedicines to enhance antitumor efficacy has become a novel strategy for cancer chemoprevention. Melanoma is the most aggressive, metastatic, and deadly disease of the primary cutaneous neoplasms. In this study, we fabricated phytoconstituent-derived zingerone nanoparticles (NPs) and validated their effects on cell adhesion and motility in melanoma B16F10 cells. Our data indicated that zingerone NPs significantly induced cytotoxicity and anti-colony formation and inhibited cell migration and invasion. Moreover, zingerone NPs dramatically interfered with the cytoskeletal reorganization and markedly delayed the period of cell adhesion. Our results also revealed that zingerone NPs-mediated downregulation of MMPs (matrix metalloproteinases) activity is associated with inhibiting cell adhesion and motility. We further evaluated the effects of zingerone NPs on Src/FAK /Paxillin signaling, our data showed that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited the protein activities of Src, FAK, and Paxillin, indicating that they play important roles in zingerone NP-mediated anti-motility and anti-invasion in melanoma cells. Accordingly, the phytoconstituent-zingerone NPs can strengthen the inhibition of tumor growth, invasion, and metastasis in malignant melanoma. Altogether, these multi-pharmacological benefits of zingerone NPs will effectively achieve the purpose of melanoma prevention and invasion inhibition.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The schematic presentation showed the effects of zingerone NPs on cell focal adhesion and cell motility in melanoma B16F10 cells.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanotechnology is a rapidly growing field that incorporates different types of materials technology and medical applications and is being used to develop new and effective treatments for diseases and cancer (Bhushan, 2017; Malik et al., 2023; Nasrollahzadeh et al., 2019; Roco, 2011; Silva et al., 2022; Whitesides, 2005). Cancer is one of the leading causes of death worldwide. Fortunately, recent advances in ameliorated methodologies and materials for many types of cancers have led to significant improvements in patient prognosis and quality of life (Hare et al., 2017; Markman et al., 2013). Plants and their derivatives have been used throughout history for treating illness and disease. Even today, despite modern chemical synthesis technology, phytochemicals continue to be the source and inspiration for new and effective drugs for the treatment of a wide range of ailments (Chin et al., 2006; Fowler, 2006). To date, many phytochemicals and herbal bioactive compounds, including phenolic and flavonoid agents, taxanes (paclitaxel), curcumins, terpenes and alkaloids, have been identified and characterized for their excellent chemopreventive properties and anticancer activities (Bilecova-Rabajdova et al., 2013; Esatbeyoglu et al., 2012; Kumar and Pandey, 2013). Therefore, natural herbal compounds have been believed to be more valuable and reliable for their superior therapeutic effects in many diseases and symptoms (such as pathogenic infection and tumorigenesis) due to the safety of herbs and lack of discernible toxicity to normal cells (Chen et al., 2011; Nobili et al., 2009; Tan et al., 2011; Treasure, 2005). In the recent decade, a novel type of green nanoparticle (NP) consisting of carbon-based nanodots has arisen and attracted a large amount of attention due to its high photoluminescence, eco-friendliness, high photostability, easy surface functionalization and/or functional decoration, and low toxicity (De and Karak, 2013; Li et al., 2022; Sahu et al., 2012). Accordingly, the combination of phytochemicals and nanotechnology to improve the unfavorable innate properties of phytochemicals and develop them into potent nanomedicines to enhance antitumor efficacy and suppress cancer metastasis has become a novel strategy for cancer chemoprevention.

Cell fate decisions, such as survival, proliferation, migration, or motility, are precisely controlled and regulated from the moment cells adhere to the surrounding extracellular matrix and are stimulated by environmental cues. These fundamental modulations are important for normal cell development, maintenance, and recovery from injury and infection. Accordingly, dysregulation of the signaling mechanisms associated with external signals and/or cues will offer cells other options for their survival, such as cell transformation, tumorigenesis, and metastasis (Burridge and Chrzanowska-Wodnicka, 1996). Cell adhesion is achieved through cooperating transmembrane integrin molecules with extracellular-matrix components such as fibronectin, vitronectin, collagen, and laminin. The clustering of integrins and their activation gives rise to the upregulation of a series of intracellular signaling pathways and molecular response activation, such as changes in intracellular pH and calcium levels, the activation of the Rho-family small GTPases and the phosphorylation of different intracellular protein-tyrosine kinases (PTKs), such as focal adhesion kinase (FAK), Src-mediated PTKs, (Giancotti and Ruoslahti, 1999; Parsons et al., 2010; Schlaepfer et al., 2004) and PI3K-mediated Akt (Weiger et al., 2009). These signaling events ultimately elicit the reorganization of the actin cytoskeleton, cell growth, and cell motility. FAK/Src/paxillin signal complexes serve as mechanosensors to modulate other adhesion protein interactions, focal adhesion turnover, actin cytoskeleton polymerization, and lamellipodia protrusion. FAK/Src/paxillin signals are therefore associated with cell adhesion strengthening and play pivotal roles in cell motility and tumor invasion (Deakin and Turner, 2008; Michael et al., 2009). Accordingly, developing therapeutic targets for Src and focal adhesion kinase may be potent strategies for antimetastatic and cancer therapy (Brunton and Frame, 2008; McLean et al., 2005; Parkin et al., 2019).

In previous studies, we prepared and demonstrated that nanosized phytochemical-derived component-zingerone NPs significantly enhanced zingerone-mediated polypharmacological effects on human hepatoma cells and human oral squamous sarcoma (Kung et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). Malignant melanoma has been demonstrated the high mortality and a lower survival rate and is a difficult disease to treat due to its pronounced highly metastatic and drug-resistant characteristics (Atallah and Flaherty, 2005). Here, to validate the roles of the phytochemically derived carbon dot-zingerone NPs-mediated anti-invasiveness and interfered with focal adhesion mechanisms in malignant melanoma, the well-characterized melanoma B16F10 cell line was chosen and employed in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zingerone NP generation and cell culture
ce:section.ce:para: The herbal aromatic component zingerone was purchased from Sigma–Aldrich (Product Number: W312401) and used without further purification. The procedure of zingerone NP fabrication has been described in a previous study (Kung et al., 2019). Briefly, zingerone was dissolved in pure ethanol (2 % w/v). Next, the zingerone solution was subjected to heat condensation at a constant temperature of 120 °C for 4 h and then stirred-cooled to room temperature. Finally, the as-synthesized zingerone NPs were passed through a 0.45 μm PVDF syringe filter without any further dialysis procedure. The constitutive structure of zingerone NPs was further characterized and speculated as a nanotetramer architecture ( Fig. 1) through several methodologies such as atomic force microscopy (AFM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), ultraviolet (UV), zeta potential, and liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) analysis (Kung et al., 2019). Murine melanoma B16F10 cells were maintained in DMEM (GIBCO™) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum (HyClone, Thermo Scientific), 2 mM glutamine, 10 µg/ml streptomycin, and 100 U/ml penicillin.

The effects of zingerone NPs on B16F10 cell viability were analyzed using a 3-(4,5-dimethyl thiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl-tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay as described in our previous study (Yang et al., 2022). Briefly, cells (1×104 cells/well) were seeded in a 96-well plate and then treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h. Next, 20 µl of MTT (2 mg/ml) was added to the cells and incubated for 90 min. Following the supernatant removal, the cellular formazan was further dissolved with DMSO (SigmaAldrich, USA), and the optical densities were determined at 570 nm using a microplate reader (BioTek EON).

The effect of zingerone NPs on B16F10 cell proliferation was assessed and monitored using a CYTONOTE 6 W time-lapse live cell imaging system (iPRASENSE). In brief, cells (2×105 cells/ml) were seeded in 6-well culture plates and incubated at 37 °C overnight. Cells were then treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs. The cell confluence was monitored and recorded at different time intervals and counted over a period of 60 h by the cell-imaging system and analyzed using HORUS Software.

The effect of zingerone NPs on melanoma cell invasion was analyzed using a Boyden chamber assay as previously described (Yang et al., 2022). Briefly, the lower chamber was filled with culture medium (30 µl/well) containing 10 % FBS. Following placement of a polycarbonate membrane (8 µm pore size Nucleopore) coated with Matrigel matrix (Corning, USA), cells (2 × 105 cells/ml) were mixed with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs and then pipetted into the upper chamber (50 µl/well). After 48 h of incubation, the membrane was fixed with methanol and stained with Giemsa solution (10 % v/v, Merck). Finally, the invasive cells were counted and photographed using an inverted microscope (ZEISS Axio Vert. A1) and counted as the mean ± SEM number of cells per filter under six different low-power fields.

The effect of zingerone NPs on colony formation was analyzed and described in a previous study (Yang et al., 2022). Briefly, B16F10 cells (2000 cells) were seeded in 6-well culture plates and incubated overnight. Cells were then treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NP for 7–10 days, ﬁxed with 4 % paraformaldehyde and stained with crystal violet solution (Sigma, St. Louis, MO) for 30 min. Next, the aggregated cells of more than 50 cells were scored as colonies and counted. Colony survival was analyzed by detecting the dissolved crystal violet supernatant from cells and/or colonies. The spectrometric absorbance was measured at 595 nm using a microplate reader (BioTek EON).

The serum-free cultured medium was collected from B16F10 cells (3×105 cells) treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NP for 24 h. A 20 µl aliquot of conditioned medium was subjected to 10 % SDSPAGE containing 0.1 % gelatin (MERCK, MILLIPORE). Following electrophoresis, the gels were washed with 2.5 % Triton X-100 buffer and reacted with activation buffer for 30 h. The gels were then stained with Coomassie blue solution as previously described (Yang et al., 2022). MMP activity was visualized as clear bands and quantiﬁed by a densitometer.

An adhesion assay was performed and modified as described previously.(Liu et al., 2006) Briefly, B16F10 cells were harvested and resuspended in medium containing various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs. Cells (2 ×105/0.2 ml) were then placed into a 48-well culture plate, which was coated with or without gelatin (0.1 %) for 1 h and incubated at 37 °C for 60 min. After incubation, the adhered cells were gently washed twice with PBS and fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde. Cells were next stained with crystal violet solution and destained with elution buﬀer. The cell adhesion capacity was measured by detecting the spectrometric absorbance at 595 nm using a microplate reader (BioTek EON). Moreover, the effects of zingerone NPs on cell adhesion were also represented with F-actin phalloidin staining images. The diameter of the cell-adhesive areas was further calculated and quantitated using ImageJ software.

The immunoblotting assay was executed as previously described (Yang et al., 2022). In brief, the protein extract (20 µg) was separated with 8–12 % SDSPAGE and transferred onto PVDF membranes (Pall Corporation). Membranes were incubated with primary antibodies recognizing proteins involved in cell migration and adhesion, including phospho-FAK (Tyr 397), phospho-c-Src (Tyr 416), phospho-Akt (Ser 473) and phospho-paxillin (Tyr 118) (Cell Signaling Technology, Inc.), paxillin, FAK, and c-Src (Santa Cruz Biotechnology) as well as antibodies recognizing cell survival indicators such as PI3K p110, PI3K p85, Akt, E-cadherin, and N-cadherin (Santa Cruz Biotechnology). Immunoreactive signals were visualized by using Immobilon Western Chemiluminescent HRP Substrate (Millipore).

Cells were seeded in a 12-well plate containing cover slides. After cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h, they were fixed with 4 % paraformaldehyde and permeabilized with 0.2 % Triton X-100 (Sigma–Aldrich). Cytoskeleton F-actin was then incubated with Alexa Fluor 488-phalloidin (Invitrogen) for 30 min, and nuclei were stained with DAPI (Sigma). Confocal images were captured on a Zeiss LSM 510 inverted laser-scanning fluorescence microscope equipped with acquisition software (LSM 510 acquisition software; Zeiss) and a 63× oil-immersion objective. Green fluorescence was acquired at 505–530 nm with excitation at 488 nm. Fluorescence images were processed with Adobe Photoshop 9.0.

Data are presented as the mean ± standard error of the mean (SEM) of the indicated experiments. The statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 5.0 software (GraphPad Software, San Diego, CA, USA). Unpaired t-tests were used to compare two groups. A P value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of zingerone NPs on cell viability, proliferation, and the in vitro tumorigenicity of melanoma B16F10 cells
ce:section.ce:para: The definition of chemoprevention is using natural, synthetic, and biological chemical agents to reverse, suppress, and prevent carcinogenic progression (Tsao et al., 2004). Because melanoma is a progressive disease, therefore, targeting different stages-associated molecular signaling or pathways in the disease using corresponding synthetic or naturally occurring chemical compounds is an ideal therapeutical strategy (Demierre and Nathanson, 2003). Melanoma should therefore suit itself well to chemoprevention.

In this study, we fabricated phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs and investigated their pharmacological effects on the malignant melanoma B16F10 cell line. We first analyzed the effects of zingerone NPs on cell viability and cell proliferation. As shown in Fig. 2, compared with zingerone, zingerone NPs significantly inhibited cell survival and altered cell morphology in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 2A). Our results further indicated that zingerone slightly elicited an increase in the cell survival rate. In contrast to zingerone-mediated cell growth, zingerone NPs significantly inhibited cell viability at 100 µM treatment, which resulted in approximately 20 % survival inhibition. Moreover, the half-maximal inhibitory concentration (IC50) of zingerone NPs was analyzed and calculated as 149.5±5.4 µM in B16F10 cells (Fig. 2B). Next, to validate the effect of zingerone NPs on cell proliferation, cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 60 h. Cell confluence was monitored, and images were taken every 6 h using a cell culture monitoring instrument (iPRASENSE) (Fig. 2C). Our data showed that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited cell proliferation in a dose- and time-dependent manner. Moreover, even at a lower dose (50 µM) of treatment, zingerone NPs induced a dramatic attenuation in cell proliferation (Fig. 2D). These results indicate that zingerone NPs markedly interfere with cell viability and inhibit cell proliferation in melanoma B16F10 cells.

Next, the effect of zingerone NPs on in vitro tumorigenicity was also verified. As shown in Fig. 3A, zingerone NPs significantly decreased colony formation in a dose-dependent manner compared to the zingerone group. Even at the lowest dose (10 µM) of treatment, zingerone NPs dramatically inhibited over 95 % of colony numbers, and doses higher than 25 µM could achieve complete inhibition of tumor colony formation compared to the zingerone group (Fig. 3B). A consistent result was also observed for colony survival (Fig. 3C). We further verified the effects of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs on the cell morphology of colonies. We found that zingerone significantly elongated the cytoskeleton with dendrite-like structures in a dose-dependent manner. Zingerone-mediated cell morphology changes by the induction of cell transformation, cytoskeleton reorganization, enlarged cell sizes, and melanin-like accumulation (over 100 μM treatment), whereas zingerone NP treatment, especially at the lower dose (25 µM), elicited the extreme elongation of cell structures and smaller cell bodies. Administration with higher doses only left destroyed cell bodies, and cell death was observed (Figure S1). These results suggested that zingerone NPs showed superior anti-cell viability and antitumorigenicity in malignant B16F10 melanoma. Moreover, zingerone NPs may disrupt cytoskeletal organization, which is involved in cell division, cell migration, cell adhesion, and cell viability.

Owing to the cell structure change reflected by zingerone NP treatment, we further analyzed the effects of zingerone NPs on cell morphology alterations. Cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h and then subjected to staining with F-actin phalloidin and imaging using confocal microscopy analysis. As shown in Fig. 4A, zingerone NPs significantly mediated the cell morphology changes in a dose-dependent manner. Moreover, distinct dendrite-like morphologies and elongated cytoskeletal structures were clearly found at the lower dose of zingerone NP (25 μM) treatment compared to the zingerone group. Further analysis of the cell architecture changes revealed that zingerone NPs mediated cell body elongation at least 2-fold compared to zingerone (Fig. 4B). This result suggests that zingerone NPs significantly disturb cytoskeletal organization and may thus affect cell viability, proliferation, and cell motility.

Indeed, previous studies have demonstrated that zingerone NPs significantly induce cytotoxicity and inhibit cell proliferation and colony formation in human hepatoma and oral cancer cells. These attributes to NPs-mediated downregulation of Akt/NFκB signaling, PI3K/Akt signaling, and inducing apoptotic caspases signaling cascades and DNA damage (Kung et al., 2023; Kung et al., 2019; Yang et al., 2022). Accordingly, Akt/NFκB signaling, PI3K/Akt signaling, and apoptotic caspases signaling cascades may be involved in zingerone NPs-mediated cytotoxicity and anti-proliferation in melanoma B16F10 cells. Additionally, cytoskeleton reorganization is pivotal in developing many pathological conditions including cancer. Maintaining cytoskeleton integrity therefore is important for resisting cell death, sustaining growth and proliferation, promoting invasion and metastasis, inducing angiogenesis, and avoiding immune response (Desouza et al., 2012; Stevenson et al., 2012; Wurzer et al., 2019; Zankov and Ogita, 2015). Here, zingerone NPs dramatically disturb B16F10 cell cytoskeletal organization by extending extremely actin filaments to affect cell proliferation and cell motility, ultimately leading to cell survival stress. Therefore, zingerone NPs have shown potent efficiency as a chemopreventive agent in malignant melanoma by interfering with cytoskeleton organization, cell proliferation, and survival activity.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zingerone NP-disturbed cell adhesion contributes to anti-invasiveness
ce:section.ce:para: Cell adhesion is a key bio-event in that cells orchestrate adhesive molecule binding to the extracellular matrix to provide structural support and chemical cues, which guide physiological and pathological processes such as cell proliferation, cell survival, and cell migration as well as invasion and metastasis (Janiszewska et al., 2020). Here, the effect of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs on cell adhesion was detected by evaluating cell anchorage onto a culture plate coated with or without gelatin. Gelatin is a natural polymer derived from collagen of ECM components (Kouguchi et al., 2017). Cells were seeded onto 48-well culture plates coated with or without 0.1 % gelatin for 60 min, and after removing the unattached cells, the remaining adherent cells were subjected to F-actin phalloidin staining and crystal violet staining assays. As shown in Fig. 5, compared to zingerone treatment, zingerone NP-mediated significantly poor adhesive ability was observed in cells cultured in either gelatin coating or not in a dose-dependent manner. Under normal culture conditions, both B16F10 cells and the zingerone group adhered to the culture plates very well within 60 min. In contrast, in the zingerone NP group, with increasing doses, the zingerone NP-mediated cell anchorage capacity worsened (Fig. 5A). Under coating conditions, the culture plates were coated with a thin layer of extracellular matrix protein-gelatin gel and establishing the interaction between cells and extracellular gelatin may require more time; thus, the cells were not well adhered and spread as under normal conditions. Nevertheless, a similar result was observed that zingerone NPs dramatically inhibited cell adhesion compared to the zingerone group. (Fig. 5B). Further analysis indicated that an effective dose of zingerone NP-mediated anti-cell adhesion was found at 100 µM treatment under either normal or gelatin-coated conditions (Fig. 5C-D). By zooming in on the images and comparing cells’ adhesive ability, we found that zingerone NPs obviously interfered with cells adhering to extracellular matrix gelatin. Furthermore, morphologically, cells were revealed to have rounded-up shapes with adherent filopodia-like structures. In the control and zingerone groups, most of the cells displayed spread-flat shapes, and some of them were observed to have adherent lamellipodia-like structures. (Figure S3). We next validated the effects of zingerone NPs on cells’ invasive ability. As shown in Fig. 6A, by using the Boyden chamber assay, we found that zingerone NPs significantly inhibited cell invasion compared to the zingerone group. Further quantitative analysis indicated that the lower dose of zingerone NPs (25 μM) displayed an obvious inhibition (approximately 40 %) of cell invasiveness compared with the same dose of zingerone (Fig. 6B). Moreover, zymography assays also demonstrated that zingerone NPs markedly attenuated MMP activity, especially that of MMP-9, compared to zingerone (Fig. 6C-D). These results suggested that zingerone NPs obviously weaken cell anchorage capacity and decreased MMPs-mediated cell invasion.

Previous studies have demonstrated that the highly invasive and metastatic characteristics of malignant melanoma are the primary reasons for poor outcomes in patients,(Switzer et al., 2022) even though there were a variety of strategies against melanoma have been developed and suggested (Eddy and Chen, 2020; Teixido et al., 2021). Accordingly, effective inhibition of cell invasiveness may be a potent strategy to attenuate melanoma metastatic activity. In the previous study, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs dramatically disturbed epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT)-related markers, such as mediating declines in the mesenchymal markers N-cadherin and vimentin rather than in the epithelial proteins ZO-1 and E-cadherin in human OSCC cell lines (Kung et al., 2019). Consistently, we also found that zingerone NPs disturbed EMT-related proteins, resulting in significantly decreased N-cadherin and limitedly affecting E-cadherin, in melanoma B16F10 cells (Figure S3). These results suggested that zingerone NPs significantly attenuated cell adhesion and may thus inhibit cell migration and cell invasion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Zingerone NP-mediated downregulation of Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling was involved in inhibited cell adhesion and cell motility and metastatic suppression
ce:section.ce:para: Cell motility depends on the reorganization of the actin cytoskeleton, which is involved in a complex interaction between the extracellular matrix (ECM) and intracellular proteins (Katoh, 2020). The Src/FAK/Paxillin complex acts as signal transduction machinery and regulates cell adhesion, cytoskeleton organization, cell migration and invasion (Turner, 2000; Webb et al., 2004). In this study, we demonstrated that zingerone NPs dramatically disturbed cytoskeleton organization and cells’ adhesive ability and inhibited cell invasiveness. Therefore, we next explored the signaling mechanism involved in cell adhesion and motility-associated proteins such as Src, FAK, and Paxillin. As shown in Fig. 7, in contrast to zingerone distinctly eliciting upregulation of Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling, zingerone NPs significantly decreased the protein activities of Src, FAK, and Paxillin through the inhibition of protein phosphorylation to suppress Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling cascades in a dose-dependent manner. Our data revealed that the Src protein was highly susceptible to zingerone NP treatment. The lower dose of zingerone NPs (50 µM) obviously decreased Src phosphorylation and protein activity compared to the zingerone group, which showed a significant decrease only at the highest dose of 200 µM (Fig. 7A-B). For FAK protein activity, zingerone NP-mediated inhibition of FAK activity was found at the higher dose of 100 µM treatment, whereas zingerone significantly elicited the upregulation of FAK phosphorylation and protein activity in a dose-dependent manner (Fig. 7C-D). Additionally, like the effect of zingerone NPs on Src protein activity, the lowest dose of zingerone NP (25 µM) treatment sufficiently inhibited 25 % of the Paxillin phosphorylation and protein activity. Following the treatment doses, the protein activity was dramatically reduced (Fig. 7E-F). These results suggested that zingerone NPs exhibited impressive inhibition of Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling cascade and thus repressed cell adhesion and cell motility.

Three types of chemopreventive agents for melanomas being suggested to target diverse situations in the population including (a.) preventing the occurrence of melanoma in healthy individuals, (b.) preventing premalignant lesions from developing into malignant melanomas, and (c.) preventing melanoma recurrence after getting treated for melanomas. (Lao et al., 2006; Shanmugam et al., 2009) On the other hand, based on the possible causes or risk factors of melanoma, the chemopreventive agents should consider their efficient actions, such as enhancing the antioxidant effect, inhibiting photo-immunosuppression, and promoting DNA damage repair, thereby preventing transformation. (Chhabra et al., 2017; Elmets et al., 2022) Accordingly, the scientific community agrees that developing chemoprevention strategies should consider the molecular basis and suitable in vitro and in vivo models during tumor progression and explore more efficient drug delivery systems (Madhunapantula and Robertson, 2012; Ravikumar and Tatke, 2019). Indeed, by nanotechnology, phytochemicals can be loaded into nano-vehicles, composed of reconstitution or surface modification to improve bioavailability and anti-melanomagenesis activity. For example, the resveratrol-lipid NPs revealed an efficient penetration of immortalized keratinocytes and inhibited cell proliferation (Teskac and Kristl, 2010). In addition, resveratrol-loaded nanocapsules have also been demonstrated to prevent metastasis and pulmonary hemorrhage in the murine melanoma model (Carletto et al., 2016). Moreover, EGCG-loaded PLGA NPs have shown their superior potential in the suppression of DNA damage in comparison to the raw EGCG in mouse skin (Srivastava et al., 2013).

In this study, we demonstrated that the herbal-derived zingerone NPs significantly inhibited Src-FAK-Paxilline signaling and blocked cell adhesion and invasion in murine malignant melanoma. Our data indicated that zingerone NPs are suited to serve as a good candidate for chemoprevention for malignant melanoma. Src, a proto-oncogene tyrosine-protein kinase and potent mitogenic and tumorigenic factor, has been shown to potentiate cell adhesion, cytoskeleton remodeling, cell invasion, and cancer progression. Src enables to induce MMPs expression for degradation of the extracellular matrix and regulates focal adhesion kinase (FAK) and paxillin for driving cytoskeleton reorganization, cell behavior and cell motility (Homsi et al., 2007). Moreover, activation of Src-mediated signaling pathways such as STAT signaling and PI3K signaling also plays pivotal roles in epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and metastasis in numerous human malignancies including melanoma (Homsi et al., 2007). In addition, interfering with the Interaction between FAK and Paxillin also shown that attenuated cell migration and invadopodia-mediated matrix degradation in metastatic melanoma cells (Hess et al., 2005; Mousson et al., 2021). Therefore, inhibition of Src-mediated signaling has been suggested as potential target in melanoma (Brunton and Frame, 2008; Halaban et al., 2019; Homsi et al., 2007).

On the other hand, PI3K/Akt signaling is a downstream signaling pathway of Src/FAK signaling and plays important roles in normal physiological and pathological processes, including cell growth, proliferation, cell motility, metastasis, and tumorigenesis (Liu et al., 2020). Here, we detected the effects of zingerone NPs on the PI3K/Akt signaling axis. As shown in Figure S4, our data revealed that zingerone NPs dramatically inhibited the protein expression levels of PI3K subunits, including the catalytic subunit p110 and regulatory subunit p85 proteins, and Akt activity in a dose-dependent manner. Our results suggested that zingerone NPs significantly repressed the PI3K/Akt signaling axis, which may be attributed to the inhibition of FAK signaling. Moreover, previous studies demonstrated that PI3K/Akt signaling could recruit guanine nucleotide exchange factors (GEFs) to activate Rho GTPases, such as Rho, Rac and Cdc42,(Barber and Welch, 2006; Weiger et al., 2009) which subsequently elicit cytoskeletal rearrangements and cellular adhesion, thereby stimulating cell migration (Ridley, 2001). The alteration of PI3K signaling frequently leads to the dysfunction of growth arrest and contributes to tumorigenesis and metastasis (Furuya et al., 2007). Therefore, downregulation of the PI3K signaling axis may effectively interfere with cytoskeleton arrangement and attenuate cell adhesion, and finally, give a rise to anti-cell motility and tumorigenesis in malignant melanoma. Indeed, several inhibitors such as BKM120, GSK2636771, and MK2206 target to PI3K/Akt pathway that have been developed and to restrain malignant cancers including melanoma (He et al., 2021). Altogether, our results suggested that zingerone NPs show superior inhibition of cell focal adhesion, cell invasion and metastasis by suppressing FAK-Src-paxillin signaling and disturbing the PI3K/Akt signaling axis. Accordingly, phytochemically derived zingerone NPs may serve as potent antagonists of cell adhesion and motility in malignant melanoma.

Suppression of cell focal adhesion is critical to interfere with cytoskeleton dynamics and cell motility, which subsequently attenuate cell growth and proliferation, cell invasion and metastasis as well as tumorigenesis. In this study, we demonstrate that phytochemical-derived zingerone NPs significantly inhibit cell proliferation and colony formation, interfering with cytoskeletal organization and attenuating cell adhesion and invasiveness. By further verifying the molecular mechanism, we reveal that zingerone NPs markedly disturb Src/FAK/Paxillin signaling transduction and downregulate PI3K/Akt signaling axis progression ( Fig. 8). These results suggested that zingerone NPs possess superior anti-focal adhesion, anti-proliferation, and anti-cell motility in melanoma B16F10 cells. Our findings will offer a novel therapeutic strategy to improve the notoriously undesirable defects in melanoma, such as high mortality, invasion, and metastasis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Figure S1. Effect of zingerone NPs on morphologies of cells within colonies of melanoma B16F10 cell line. Effect of zingerone NPs on cellular morphologies of colonies in melanoma B16F10 cells. After cells (2000 cells/well) were incubated with various concentrations of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 7–10 days, the cell morphologies of colonies were imaged and recorded using an inverted microscopy assay.

Figure S2. Effects of nanosized-zingerone on cell adhesive ability toward different extracellular matrix coating conditions. Cells were suspended in a medium containing HAS and/or HAS NPs and seeded in a culture plate coated with or without gelatin and/or collagen for 60 min. Cells were then stained with F-actin phalloidin and then analyzed the adhesive ability using inverted fluorescent microscopy. (A) Bright phase images, and (B) F-actin phalloidin stained images. White arrows indicated the cell’s well-flat and spreading morphology, and yellow arrows indicated the cell’s filipodia-like structures which implied cells trying to adhere and anchor with the extracellular matrix.

Figure S3. Effect of zingerone NPs on the expression of EMT markers in B16F10 cells. Cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h, cells were then harvested and subjected to detect the EMT-related proteins including E-cadherin and N-cadherin using Western blotting assay. Data were represented and executed in at least three separate experiments. *, P<0.05; **, P<0.01.

Figure S4. Zingerone NPs inhibited the PI3K/Akt signaling axis involved in the suppression of cell motility. (A) Cells were treated with various doses of zingerone and/or zingerone NPs for 24 h, cells were then harvested and subjected to detect the protein expression profiles of PI3Ks and Akt using Western blot assay. (B) Proteins expressed levels were next quantitated and analyzed using GraphPad Prism 5.0 software. Data were represented and executed in at least three separate experiments. *, P<0.05; **, P<0.01.
